definition: (common expression of negation) 1. (before adjectives, verbs and other adjectivally used words) not ... (ModJ ora, mal. tidak). 2. (before nouns or substantivally used words) it is not ... ; not being ... (ModJ dudu, mal. bukan). 3. (followed by an arealis it functions as prohibitive particle) do not ... ! (= haywa + realis; ModJ aja, mal.jaṅan).

65.2: Passim. Found in a number of compounds e.g. cakrawartitanaya, narendra-, nṛpa-, Pāṇḍu-, rāja#etc. A problem is the frequent use of tanayan in the same meaning:

definition: 1. special sign on a banner belonging to each hero, ensign, standard, banner 2. a category of dignitaries or officials. Is it (originally): in charge of a ``banner'' or company? It seems, however, that it does not always point to a military rank. Pigeaud renders it with ``headman''. Is it distinguished from mantri? But taṇḍa-mantri, certainly in catus-taṇḍa-mantri, denotes one rank or dignity (chief officer?). See also catus-, pañca-.

definition: (to be read tantu?) thread, warp? See under tantu 1.

(Skt warp; the leading or essential part, main point, characteristic feature, model, framework; doctrine, rule, scientific work; a class of works teaching magical and mystical formularies, mostly in the form of dialogues between Śiwa and Durgā; ifc. row, number, series, troop). See also adṛśya-, asura-, dīkṣā-, jyotiṣa-, kāma-, kula-, kusumāyu#dha-, madana-, mānawa-, rodra-, sarwa-, smara-, su-, swa-, wyākaraṇa-. 1. doctrine, esp. magical-mystical doctrine and formularies (mantras) (cf aji) 2. illustrative stories (of the nītiśāstra) 3. line, lineage; he who propagates the succession (cf tantu), ancestor (see -waṅśa, yukti, kula-, su-)

definition: 1. (Skt) conjecture, guess, opinion; reasoning, inference 2. definition: (Skt) ``thatness'', what makes something what it is, being, reality. See also acintya-, andha-, anitya-, apsu-, ārādhana-, asādhya-, buddhi-, bhuwana-, dharma-, graha-, guṇa-, jagat-, kewala-, kṣīrārṇawa-, maṅgala-, nimna-, parama-, paramārtha-, paramaśiwa-, parwa-, pradhāna-, prāṇa-, pūrwa-, sadā-, śiwa-, samādhi-, sarwa-, sarwajña-, śiwa-, smara-, sūkṣma-, śūnya-, triguṇa-, weda-, wighnārtha-, yoni-. 1. what something in fact is 2. the actual facts of the matter, how it really happened, the true story; story 3. the essential, the actual (as contrasted with the apparent or incidental). Sometimes tattwa is the concrete object in its essence, katattwan the abstract essence of the concrete object. 4. the various categories of reality according to Sāmkhya doctrine 5. doctrine concerning reality, philosophy; the writings containing this doctrine 6. really, in reality? as is said?

sub_entries.definition: to assume the reality of, form the essence of

definition: a pointed weapon for stabbing. It is hard to tell whether perhaps one part. type of weapon is meant; it occurs where, elsewhere or in the same context, pĕḍaṅ or kris is used, but it is also found in an enumeration alongside pĕḍaṅ or kris. See also tuhuk III.

5.6: tiga-mata sira muṅgah ṅkāne rĕṅga gajapati. Pb. renders: Indra. Even if tigamata is a possible rendering of Skt trinetra, it remains strange as a name for Indra. Skt trinetra (-nayana, etc) is always Śiwa. Is mata emphatic particle and then ``the three'': Kāma, Indra and Wṛhaspati?

definition: The base-word occurs as imperative, as standing for tumon after n, yan, etc, and in the compound salah ton (``with a wrong sight or view, being mistaken''). Apart from that, ton is often found in a context where it seems to stand for tumon, especially at the beginning of a verse-line (see what is said s.v. sambut); occasionally also for katon, or perhaps as a sort of interjection: look! lo and behold!

34.6: garwa manon musuh. Anon, tumon, etc passim in all texts. Manon (saṅ manon, saṅ hyaṅ manon) is used for the divine element (ātma, etc) which may be tan katon (sūkṣma, niṣkala) or katon (mūrti, sakala):

points to the fact that tuñjuṅ in Kuntiyajña is the way they follow to the underworld to look for Arjuna: sĕkar niṅ sarasija punika dadalan iṅsun, apan trus mariṅ saptapatala kājaranya liṅ niṅ sastra. (1447) 12B: acadar atwih amanantĕn; Pg 9a.30: mapadati sataṅkilan, acadar rwaṅ pacadaran, atwih pataṅ wide. In the inscr. it is mentioned among trades and crafts. AgP refers to people who are reborn as paśu, sapi, kĕbo and then bought by atwih. The slaughtering is done by a caṇḍāla, after which the meat is sold by the atwih. It may be the man who keeps the bullocks, used for the bullock-cart. In this case wĕḍai (wiḍe, waḍai) must be the place where these animals are kept. It is also possible, however, that atwih is the man who prepares hides, a tanner.
